video_title: Abigail Wattley – Inside the $4.5B Williams Endowment | Capital Allocators EP.507
transcript: [music] >> Abigail, thanks so much for joining me. >> Oh, it's so great to be with you today. Thanks, Ted. >> I would love you to take me through the highlights of your career, which I know aren't going to take that long in terms of steps, and then we can dive in from there. >> Well, so I graduated from Williams in 2005, and from there was very lucky to get a position at Cambridge Associates right out of Williams. And interestingly, I had gotten interested in the economics of higher education as a senior in college. I was really fortunate to get to take a tutorial with our then president of the college, which is a gentleman by the name of Morty Shapiro. And he was an economist, and so that really opened my eyes to this whole world of higher education and finance. And so that's what interested me in going to Cambridge Associates. I was there for 2 years, and I like to say kind of right place, right time. I was very fortunate to be a very junior analyst that happened to be staffed on the Williams account. And it happened to overlap with the period of time where the college had made a choice to hire its first dedicated chief investment officer, of course, Klitch Shelton. And so when she came on board, um I started working pretty closely with her. She didn't have much of a team at the time. I think if you listen to her podcast, you'll know I think there was a joke about her having a computer and a phone and not much else. So, got to work really closely with her. And so when she finally had office space in kind of spring of 2007, I ended up joining her here at the Williams Investment Office. And have basically been here ever since except for a 2-year break for business school. >> Take me through 20 years of growth inside the office. What you knew, what you came to learn along the way about the investment process and you know, researching managers, everything. >> Yeah, I mean, it's a tremendous amount of growth both for me personally as well as for our portfolio and what it is that we do today. So, there's a there will be a lot to unpack there. But, when we first got here back in 2007, you know, the history of the office prior to that was everything was managed by a volunteer committee. And so, really early on, one of the things that we did was to put in place a policy portfolio. And there were a lot of manager decisions that we had to go through. And I So, for me being here even as an analyst early on was just such an eye-opening experience um getting to see, you know, we did everything from a $400 million transition from a separately managed account that we had to stuffing the FedEx envelopes and everything in between those two tasks. And I remember setting up a lot of the performance reports. Frankly, some of them we still use today. So, it's really fun to know, you know, sometimes people will say to me, you know, this is how this report works and I just sort of chuckle and say, yep, I'm sure it works still exactly the same. I know what the ins and outs are, but um we have just I think continued to develop as an investment organization so much during that time. Um I think when we first started, it was 1.3 billion. Today, we're about 4.5 billion in assets. And we've just accrued a lot of knowledge over time about what works for the portfolio. We've made a lot of mistakes, but we've also made, you know, some really, really good decisions along the way. >> If you look at your first couple of years and just take the the unit of researching a manager, thinking about a decision, what did you know or not know that you kind of learned in the next phase? So, step by step all the way through. >> Well, I didn't know a lot early on personally. And I remember so I think I mentioned I was here for a year before I went to business school. So I came back to the investment office in 2010. And I remember sitting down with Colette. I'd gone out to lunch with her. It was my second year of business school and I had a couple job offers and I was actually genuinely sitting down to ask her advice about which one I might take. And she said, "Well, I'd really like for you to come back to the investment office. Would you consider doing that?" And I said, "Yes, I'd love to." And she said, "Okay, why don't you write the job description for the job that you'd like to do and then we'll talk about it." And I remember going home and writing that job description and there were I think probably three key things that were on that job description. One was I wanted to own asset classes and I'll come back to what those were cuz that's really the answer to your question. Two is I wanted to manage people. Um so that's hence the analyst program. And three was I wanted to report to Colette cuz I knew that was going to really be my opportunity to learn a lot. But the going back to my first point around the asset classes that I wanted to manage, I think what she basically did was went to the two managing directors at the time. Um one on the marketable, one on the non-marketable side and said, "Okay, which asset class do you want to give up?" And so I like to think that I ended up with great asset classes, but I think they were basically the ones nobody wanted which meant that I was managing investment grade fixed income and real assets. And so to answer your question, I really had frankly not a lot of background in either of those areas of the market and actually those are really technical asset classes when you start to peel back the layers of the onion. And so I had a lot to learn and I think that was frankly one of my greatest lessons was figuring out how to really dive deep and learn an area of the market. And so it started with a lot of conferences. I remember going to a PIMCO bond boot camp for a week. They used to run this amazing boot camp, and I spent a week out in California, and Bill Gross was there, and they had all their senior PMs basically like teaching us everything there was to know about bonds. So, that was my indoctrination into the world of fixed income. And then similarly with oil and gas, you know, I I grew up in New England, right? I really didn't know what was going on. So, that meant I needed to go spend a lot of time in Texas, right? And so, there also was this great program down I think it was at SMU, and they used to call it oil and gas investing for non-petroleum engineers. And it was a week-long course, and it was the same kind of thing where we would sit in in a classroom, we'd look at well reports, they would basically tell us what we're supposed to be looking at. I learned a ton from that, and from networking, there were so many great people along the way who were really established in those asset classes that really, you know, helped me along. And so, yeah, that was really my first experience kind of how did how to dig in in a market, how to dig in on managers, and it set me up for kind of what I ended up doing later. >> Once you built that technical knowledge, you could say even in those two asset classes, and then started applying it to understanding who's good in the space, what did you think you know, and then what did you come to learn about managers to get to the point where you were comfortable that you felt like you knew what you were doing? >> One of the biggest lessons that I learned, and somebody said this to me along the way, and I really try to think about it a lot, is that you can teach people what they're supposed to do, but it's really hard to teach people what they're not supposed to do without them actually making the mistakes themselves. And I think maybe one of the things that I'm very grateful for was how long of a leash I had to kind of figure out some of the mistakes and that's maybe where I had the greatest lessons, but I don't know. There's so many different things that I would say. Um, you know, one of them is when it's very clear that there's something great, chances are good you should just go for it. And in this business, we don't get points for difficulty. We get points for making money, right? And so, you don't necessarily have to always go find the least discovered idea. Um, if somebody's recommending a manager to you over and over again, for example, like just probably like worth taking that advice and at least spending a lot of time on it. Um, so that might have been one of my lessons learned. I think the other thing is real assets was not a place where quite as many people were spending a ton of time, and so the managers were so generous with me and the time that they gave me, and it really got me comfortable like sitting in a conference room for hours upon hours upon hours and just constantly asking as many questions as I possibly could. And they were all so patient with me, and that really taught me a lot, too. So, those are maybe some of my lessons is, you know, not being afraid to ask questions, to take their time when you need it. Um, and you're going to make some mistakes. >> How did Collette work with you to allow you to make mistakes, but then not have it impact a portfolio when there aren't really that many decisions that get made? >> She was always really good at like I would do a lot of the legwork, right? The sourcing, the landscaping, the networking, coming up with a short list of ideas, and she always met with every single one of our managers before they went into the portfolio, and she was very good that one of the one of the many things she was very good at is whenever you put a manager in front of her, you know, she would flip to immediately like page 47 of I don't know how she developed this instinct, but she could get to page 47 within the first 5 minutes and be like this is the issue, right? And so she would come with me kind of for those final diligence meetings and that's really where a lot of I would get a lot of feedback on, you know, this we're probably going to lean in this direction, right? So she would kind of towards the end of the process I think really help drive the direction of where we were going and it would that taught me a lot. >> How did you evolve from covering these two asset classes to broadening across portfolio? >> So from there I think probably the biggest next step that I took was ultimately taking over our hedge fund asset classes which for those that maybe know less about the Williams portfolio, those have always been really important asset classes for our portfolio. We've been long-term investors in hedge funds and they represent I think at the time they might have represented 40% of the portfolio. But again, I think because I had built a little bit of a muscle around how to learn to do something new, right? Stepping into the complexity of long short equity and absolute return, that was again kind of that same opportunity to figure out, okay, how do I unpack this whole landscape? How do I get to know these managers? There's some more sensitivities, right? In hedge funds around like how much time you're taking with managers and things like that. So I think I had to I had to learn to sharpen my diligence process a little bit which was probably a really good evolution. It was a very different network, too. I had to there were new people that I needed to talk to both from the LP community as well as the GP community and that also really expanded my horizons. >> So in the years before you took over as CIO, what changed in the thinking on the investment portfolio here? >> It's interesting you ask that cuz we're doing we're in the midst of our asset allocation work right now. And so it's always a really good opportunity to stop and reflect. And I have been looking a lot at the portfolio over time. And what's interesting to me is the actually the big blocks of what we do really haven't changed, right? There are on the margin of course changes that we're making every year. But I think one of the things that has worked really well for Williams is that we've been extremely consistent in how we deploy the portfolio, what works for Williams, and really keeping at the center what we do, why is it that we're here? And we're we're very clear-eyed about that. You know, our job is to earn a 5% real return, to make sure that we're doing that with the appropriate level of risk that we're taking, and ensuring that we have sufficient liquidity because we support nearly 60% of the operating budget for the college. And so yes, those numbers have changed on the margin like the the percentage of the operating budget support, for example, but our goals have never changed. And so in some ways the way that we pursue that has always remained very consistent. And I think that's a real source of strength for us. >> How do you think about your strategy and almost like your right to win as an LP? What do you bring to the table as the next LP in a fund that you're interested investing in? >> There are a couple different things that we will talk about with a manager while we're meeting with them and and hopefully getting access to something that may be more access constrained. So one is the mission, right? That really does resonate with people. Williams is extremely generous with its financial aid program. We have no loans in our financial aid program at this point in time because of the generosity that comes from the endowment and our donors. So, that resonates with people. But then beyond that, you know, I think we manage a really nice size of assets at 4 and 1/2 billion. We can have a meaningful position in our portfolio without overwhelming a manager and still be meaningful partners in both directions. And so, I think that can also really resonate. And then really importantly, we have an amazing team here. And I know you've had a chance to meet many of them. But they are really smart and really thoughtful. And I think sometimes people just like sitting across the table from them, right? We show up to meetings, we're prepared. We ask careful, thoughtful questions. We We want to be on the same team as our managers. And I think that that approach really can resonate. >> When it came time for a Collette to step away, retire, and you've been in that seat for a long time. I'm curious what the lead-up to that in your becoming the CIO look like from your perspective. >> You know, it's so funny because now reflecting, you know, there are ways in which it went really fast and also really slow at the same time. I think sometimes like parenthood, right? Where you're like the days are long the whatever that saying is. But probably one of my favorite preparation points like when what it was public that this transition was happening. And which I think probably got announced like in January or February and Collette was retiring June 30th. And we went out to California and we spent close to a week together basically meeting all of our managers, anybody that I hadn't spent quite as much time with particularly on the private side of the portfolio. And she was making sure that she had done a very careful, clean handoff. And just that opportunity to kind of to spend that time with her and really now be in a place where there were questions that I could ask her that maybe I hadn't before and some of the, you know, anxieties that I could share with her and we could really talk through some of those things was just like really one of my probably favorite transition points and there were a lot of different things, right? That we had to transition, but manager relationships are so important to what we do and I think the care and thoughtfulness in which she worked through that was really valuable. >> You know, a lot of transitions we see, you don't necessarily see the internal candidate become the next CIO. I'd love to get your perspective on the value of duration. Having been here for 20 years now becoming the CIO, what are some of the things that you think Williams has benefited from because of the duration of the team? >> I think it actually goes back to something I said earlier, which is that I think we've been clear-eyed about what our strategy is since day one and that hasn't changed. And so by having an internal candidate become the CIO, we can stay laser-focused on what it is that we're here to do and um we didn't have to take there was no time wasted or lost in sort of repointing the ship. And, you know, that I think is a huge huge huge benefit. I think I also coming into the role, you know, knew the portfolio, especially some of our like really important long-standing relationships, kind of the role that they played, the value that they added, and the fact that those were not going to change anytime soon. And so that was like having some clear things that we weren't going to be changing was very valuable. That being said, you know, on the flip side, there were a couple new people that joined kind of as I became CIO and one of the things that I think is really powerful right now about our team is that we have a lot of people sitting around this big table that you and I are sitting around that bring really different experience with them from other investment offices. And so we sort of have this really interesting powerful combination of we understand and respect the history of the portfolio and why we've been able to generate the returns that we have over time and what's really important to stay focused on and yet we're really not stagnant in the work that we're doing. And I think that pairing is really important and one of the things that's been very fun for me in having this team is the ability to learn from them and to kind of draw from them the best practices that they bring to the table from the different places that they have all worked. >> What are some examples of things that were eye-opening for you that other people brought in from their past experiences? >> Yeah, I mean I think like one example that just comes to mind is I spent very little time previously on biotech as an opportunity set. And both of the managing directors Julia and Paul have a lot of experience investing in that space. And so that was an an area where pretty early on they were both like saying, "Okay, we probably need some you know, we could really lean in here. There's some interesting opportunities." And they both had well-developed lists of high-quality managers that we should be speaking to. And in that moment I was sort of the least experienced one at the table and um but I in some ways loved that because it was such an opportunity for our portfolio. We had to strike a balance of not doing you know, too much too quickly and being careful, but on the flip side, it's really exciting when there's an area for us that we can really go do a lot of good work. So, that's an example. I think also, there's just been a lot of interesting process improvements that we've been able to adapt from everybody's different experiences. And so, which has really had the added benefit of helping us know our portfolio even better, know our managers better, know what we hold. And so, I think this is a culture that really embraces technology, for example, and that's been wonderful to just sort of embrace that and really improve things. >> What have you done differently with tech? >> So, I don't know that it's like revolutionary, but you know, we have a lot better transparency now, like look-through transparency on the portfolio, kind of at our fingertips, um which is really helpful when we're thinking about exposures, what do we own, what do we maybe not own that we should own more of, right? So, that's been very helpful. We've also adopted, this is also maybe not revolutionary, but it's has saved the team a ton of time, where we've been able to sort of automatically download and organize all of our data from all of our different managers, right? That we used to have probably a person and a half that might have, you know, worth of people time doing that kind of work. And we have 350 different line items in this portfolio, you can imagine how much content gets posted to Intralinks. And to not be spending time on those things is really valuable because that that means we're spending more time reading and analyzing than just compiling. >> Khrisha, is there anything that other other perspectives that other team members brought in about specific managers that you feel like you might have missed just cuz you've been there for a a time? >> Well, that has been really interesting, right? We have really re-underwritten the entire portfolio because you have to when you have new people coming on board and taking over responsibility for asset classes. And that has also been very humbling for me, right? Some of these were asset classes that I might have um originally built up and when you look at some of these things through the eyes of another person, you do realize there are certain places where maybe you have gotten a little bit more complacent or you've let the story run a little bit too long. And so, I think we have them making a lot of really good changes. The other thing I will say and what's interesting to me is we all come from different backgrounds, but in some ways very philosophically similar backgrounds, right? And in terms of the kinds of managers we have historically gravitated to. But I think again, because we have such a great team here, there's a lot of safety in thinking about new things. And so, what that has allowed us to do is look at opportunities that we might not have historically. So, things for example, like in the market neutral space or some of the more quantitative strategies which were not historically represented in our portfolio, at least getting the comfort to say we should be really smart about this. Not to say we have to do it, but we should know the space and we should be smart about whether or not maybe there is something to be doing here. And it's not that any one person brought a lot of experience there, but I think we complement each other well enough to be willing to go do that work. >> I'd love to double click on, let's just say hedge funds. What do you think today about the role of hedge funds that said at least once were 40% of what you're doing and how you're participating in that space? >> Hedge funds are no longer 40%, but they're still meaningful. Um so, I think today our target allocation across our long short and absolute return portfolio was 27% in aggregate. So, so we're a believer. Would be my first comment. They have been a very important part of our portfolio not only for meeting that return objective that I mentioned, but they do so while maintaining our liquidity and also helping us to manage volatility. And so, we really through cycles have seen that play out for the portfolio and again, when you're you're spending 5% of the portfolio every year, all three of those mandates do really matter. And I think that's sometimes something that maybe gets a little bit lost when people talk about returns, right? Um, is that yes, if if the goal of this endowment was to just put it in a drawer and not spend from it for 10 years, you might do something very differently than if you actually knew you had to draw 5% from it every year. And I think in that context, hedge funds make a lot of sense. They've obviously gone through many different cycles and periods where they've done better or worse. But again, this is where I think Williams is very fortunate in that we've had these long-standing relationships. We have the confidence to see through cycles and we have some amazing manager names that have have done really well for the portfolio. >> How do you think about balancing the desire for that type of return profile and the known high costs of doing business when if you look back at 20 years ago, it sure looked a lot easier for hedge funds to generate what you needed for the portfolio. >> Well, I think the big question is whether or not that's kind of come back in style right now, right? Like if volatility is back and the ability to really think about things both long and short may be back and rates are no longer zero, so that's also helpful. We always look at things net of fees, right? So, that's kind of always the starting point is we don't think of them like from a cost perspective as the fees if they net of fees they justify a role in the portfolio, then we are really happy to have them and there is some really good talent, right? It's It's It would be hard to think that for any asset cuz I know asset classes go in and out of style a lot. But, what you really have to ask yourself in that context is like did all the talent leave suddenly or is this maybe just a more difficult period of time? And so, you know, hedge funds is a really big monolith by the way. Like that's So, um there's definitely plenty of talent still and and our job is to just make sure that that's what we're accessing. >> A lot of discussion these days about private market liquidity and where people are sitting and future returns. I'd love to get your perspective on how you're thinking about that. >> We talk about it all the time as a team. We talk about it with our amazing investment committee. Again, I think this is an example of where because our portfolio is very set cycle tested, um we are comfortable waiting and being really really really patient. And so, from our perspective, there is no like big headline change in how we're thinking about the role of private asset classes. Where it does change a little bit is things like our modeling as it relates to our commitment pace modeling and if the expectation is going to be that things may be less liquid for a little bit longer, do we need to maybe modestly slow down commitments um or just think about kind of how big those asset classes could become kind of stress testing some of those scenarios. So, I think big picture we're very committed to the asset classes with the caveat that we're quite aware that timelines could be a little bit extended and we just want to be really mindful about what that means from a portfolio construction perspective. >> When you run through that math exercise, where do you come out on the other end of like how much you want to reduce your commitment pacing on a run rate basis? >> Well, it's interesting and tricky because one of the things that really drives that too is how much growth do you expect from the overall pool, right? And so even what's interesting is even when we have pushed out like, okay, the expectation is distributions might be further out into the future, when you pair that with but this is the expected growth of the pool over time, it's not as dramatic as you would think. We we've seen a little bit of a decline in the venture portfolio. Um the model we're working on right now is actually telling us we still need to be stepping up commitments and buyouts, interestingly, which is a point of big discussion within the team. Like, is that right? Do we think we have enough, you know, good assumptions in this model? Um But again, the other thing I will always say is the models are guidelines, right? We're super mindful of the fact that we are not filling buckets. And so the model may tell us we need to do a $150 million of commitments this year, but if we don't see opportunity that matches that level, we're just not going to do it. And we also really try to think about things in sort of the average over the next 3 years um versus like any 1 year's commitment budget. So, it's always a moving target. We get to adjust it a lot. It's responsive to the market. But I think the headline numbers are are bigger and so with that is a question mark for us. >> It's been a there's a lot of successes in the venture world that are still sitting as very large late stage privates that filter into, I'm sure, your portfolio. How have you thought about that group of companies that have been winners from venture investments that you don't have liquidity on today? >> I think big picture is again, this is where we're fortunate to be long-term investors, where we can be really patient. And for most of those companies, where we agree with our venture managers that they're still compounding, right? So, whether or not they're publicly traded or not, you know, they're still great companies and we're going to benefit long-term from their continued growth. It has had, I think, an impact on our asset allocation, right? So, if you look at one of the things that we did do over the last few years was increase our target to buyouts and venture. And there were there were a number of reasons for that, but I think part of it is in recognition of the fact that there are a lot of companies that are just staying private for a longer and they're going to just compound in your private portfolio. And what's also interesting about that, though, is whether or not you actually are still getting venture-like returns on those investments or are they something different? As long as they're equity returns, I think we're generally comfortable because what most important is we want to make sure we have a significant enough weight to equity, very broadly defined, for that growth component. But it has had some impact on both the allocation and then what you you're expected return might be from those asset classes. >> How have you thought about liquidity in the context of the whole portfolio? >> We think about it so much. I think this is one of the things that is I don't know I don't know if I would say we're we are really good at, we're very tuned to. And again, this is, I think, something Colette taught us very well, which is our number one purpose for being here is to support the college, period, end of story. There is no question that that is what we need to be able to do. And so, not having enough liquidity is not an option. And so, we obsess over our liquidity position, by which I mean it is one of the key topics we cover every week as part of our team meeting. We are regularly going over every line item that comes in and out of the portfolio every week. We know what the forecast is for the next 12 months for every asset class and where the dollars are going and importantly where the money's going to come from to support the college rates. And then we're also as part of that stress testing it regularly. We also maintain $150 million worth of lines of credit for the investment office so that if we These are really belt and suspenders, but if we needed it, we would have kind of a backup source of liquidity. Um but we're very fully invested. We only maintain between 1 and 2% of the portfolio in cash. So that's why we have to be really planful about our liquidity, but I think that's a really important advantage long term, right? Because it means that the portfolio is in the markets and working. And when you have a team of 11 people as we do here, one of the things that we can be spending time on and thinking about is that liquidity and where is it going to come from and planning for it. And so it kind of becomes a win-win um when we make that a priority. >> If you go back to your original domains post business school investment grade bonds and real assets, >> They've changed a lot. >> [laughter] >> That's the question. How How have they How's your thinking changed about their use in the portfolio? >> so much. Well, just one thing that was so interesting I was reflecting um in getting ready for this conversation, but I remember it was August of 2011. Do you remember when the US got downgraded? And and I was managing an investment grade fixed income portfolio and that was like that my first moment of panic of realizing like oh no, I I'm probably supposed to know what this is going to mean for the portfolio and I think it happened on a Friday night and I remember being kind of in the office. This was before people did work remotely. And so being in the office kind of all weekend trying to figure out like what is this going to mean for our portfolio. It turns out it didn't mean much, but we've been on a really interesting episodic journey with investment grade fixed income. It came out of the portfolio entirely, I think in 2019, um if I'm recalling correctly, because uh not that we have we don't take a view on interest rates. I think that's really important. But as one of our committee members likes to say, there was a higher probability that rates were going to go up than they were going to go down. And so it's not having a view, it's just understanding what the probabilities are. We did reintroduce it about a year ago um as rates kind of came off of zero, but it's still a really modest position. It's 1% position in our portfolio and it's really meant to be that rainy day. It's not a big piece of what we do anymore. It used to be like 10% of the portfolio back in back in fixed income heyday. So it's definitely changed a lot. And then real assets, similarly to your question, we no longer do funds dedicated to the extraction of oil and gas. And so what does it mean to manage a real assets portfolio in that context has really had to change and evolve and I think honestly we're still trying to figure that out. >> [snorts] >> Because I'm a believer. I've seen that that asset class work over time and I do think it can be really diversifying for a portfolio. And so we are not abandoning the strategy, but we have had to be really thoughtful about what it actually means in today's context. And we have a couple managers that we like. Um we're doing things in power and some renewable infrastructure and things of that nature, um which we're really excited about, but we have to keep pushing and figuring out what it means. >> Once you came into the CIO seat, what was different from what you thought in all the preparation that you had going in? >> I feel, forgive me the analogy, I think one of the things that reminded me the most of was actually becoming a parent. Because it's the kind of thing where people can tell you what it's going to be like or you think you know what it's going to be like and the reality of it is still entirely different when somebody hands you a child and says like this is yours, good luck. And so that maybe was like that initial feeling um and yet I also felt again so fortunate that I knew the portfolio, I knew our committee, I knew the college and I knew the team and I still sort of felt like oh wow, like this is this is new and different. Um so again, I really I'm very impressed with people who take on this seat and don't have all of that context. Um it is very impressive to me. So that was probably like just the like it is different when at the end of the day, you know, you are the person with whom the responsibility sits and early on I think one of the things that becomes challenging but you eventually learn to like deal with is the number of decisions that come your way and I I would just remember early on being like really this is this is my decision too and this one and like some of them are teeny tiny and some of them are really big and everything in between and early on even the smallest decision can feel very consequential because you feel like you're this is you're establishing the expectations, the path, the process, whatever it is and so you think about things a lot and it it's exhausting. Um but eventually you've set the path and so some of those little decisions weigh on you a lot less and you can really start to focus on the big ones. But I think all of those things it's somebody can tell you that that's how it's going to be and again that just the reality of actually having to hold that on your shoulders is different. >> If you put the lens back on of learning by making mistakes, what are some of the mistakes you think you made coming into the CIOC? >> I don't want to say it's a mistake, but it's it's a piece of advice that I will credit to our current investment committee chair, who's amazing, Nate Sleeper, who said to me early on, because you know, one of the things that I was really wrestling with was this tension between respecting our history and understanding that part of the reason that they were interested in promoting an internal candidate was they liked what was happening, and I did too, and we had a great track record, and so how do you stay really respectful to all of that, and yet also kind of make your mark and do things your way, and that kind of the tension between the two and what's the right balance. That that is a really there's no right answer to that. You have to feel your way kind of through that. And I remember saying at one point to Nate, like if I'm going too fast with anything, like will you tell me, like make sure I you're telling me if that's the case, and he said, I I don't think that's going to be the case, and more likely than not, you're going to look back at kind of where you are after 3 years and say, gosh, I wish I had done some of these things sooner. And so I do think as I'm coming up to July 1st will be 3 years, and I'm so happy with where we sit today, but even some of the things that we have done, I'm like, oh, if we could only have done that even faster, that would have been great. So, it's not necessarily a mistake as much as it is, like I I think there is a you have to have some time to build your confidence. So, I don't know that I could have gone back and done it differently, but that would have been it's okay to, you know, do things the way that you want to do them. >> What are some examples of things you now say, I wish I knew to move a little faster? >> I think so, like some of the technology that we adopt adopted within the port within the process, we could have done even faster, and certainly that's even like now with AI, like top of mind for me cuz I'm trying to figure out how do we do that at the right speed. And there's some changes to the portfolio that I think we were really careful about making. Again, it's hard for me to say like what I definitely have done done it all differently, but there are a couple more changes to the portfolio that I'd like to make, and um part of me wishes like we had done it a quarter or two earlier. >> Let's ask you a bit about running a team. >> Yes. >> You mentioned early on being in charge of an analyst program and wanting to manage people. Now you've got a couple of new newer senior people come in. How do you think about being a leader of a team and and training people in the in the business? >> Honestly, it's one of my favorite parts of the job is getting to work with our team. I I think I've mentioned, but I will say it again cuz it's true, we have an amazing team here. And they're really smart, really dedicated, um really focused, and so it is a really interesting challenge for me to figure out how do I stay relevant to them, and how and value add to the process. And that's actually, you know, maybe even going back to one of your earlier questions, right? What is the role as CIO that I really want to play? Um and I don't know if this analogy will resonate or not, but I because we have such a strong team, like one of the things that I've come to realize that is so valuable about my time is the opportunity to really be able to think more broadly about the portfolio, and my analogy is that I like to think that it's a little bit of like being a snowplow and getting out on the streets before everybody else is awake, and making sure that the streets that you think your team is going to want to go down, like you've had a chance to just like check out and make sure they're clear, and that they can run as fast as they want to run and you're happy for them to be on those roads and um and that you've gone around the corner, right? So, that's kind of like a little bit of how I think about it. Um and then and at the same time you're kind of also like making sure like kind of running back to the rear, too, and making sure like did everything like that I thought was getting done really keep moving forward in the right direction. But, we really value, I guess to the other part of your question, the opportunity to develop talent, right? So, that was our first analyst dedicated analyst program started back in 2010. We also have a winter study class that we teach on campus. We also have a summer internship program that we run through the investment office. We now have over 150 students that have participated between one of our three programs, which is amazing. And so, we like to say we eat our own cooking. We believe in young talent and we want to be part of developing people for the future. And so, whether it's programmatically with our analysts and or more broadly with kind of like our more experienced members of our investment team, I think what we're very good about is kind of communicating on a one-on-one basis with people what is it that you're interested in, how can we make sure that your opportunity is you your learning curve stays really steep. And if it's feeling like it's flattening, what else can we do to help make sure that we're steepening it again. Um and so, I just I spend a lot of time with members of our team kind of in check-ins and updates. And we have a of course rigorous performance process management process. All of those, you know, I think good practices. We really try to do well by them. >> If you look back at the 150 people that have been through this program starting with 16 years ago, where are they in the industry generally speaking? >> All over. We did this analysis a couple years ago and I should update it, but I think at the time our data told us about 50% of them stay in asset management broadly defined and 50% go on to do other amazing things, which is fantastic, too. But the 50% that kind of stay in asset management broadly defined are at other LPs, which is really fun for us to get to network with them. Some of them are at other GPs, which again is really useful and interesting to us. So, they're kind of all over the industry right now. >> How do you think about the blend of cooperation and competition with peers? >> We tend to lean more on the cooperative side, in part because I think we can at our size. It's not as if we are going to block somebody out from an opportunity or they're going to block us out. Again, this kind of goes back to we can still be meaningful at a reasonable check size. And we're very cognizant of the fact that we are a small team. We're seven investment people covering the global opportunity set. And so, when we can benefit from another person's perspective, we really are happy to take the input, the advice, but you know, that's not a one-way street. You have to be willing to share. And so, I think on the margin we would prefer to be cooperative and to gain, you know, learn from other people's experiences and we're competitive [clears throat] in that we want to produce an excellent, excellent return for Williams and we know how that stacks up relative to our peers and we want it to look great, but we also believe we can do that by, you know, with some cooperation. >> What are some of the ways you've tried to enhance returns beyond the core structure of find a bunch of managers and stay with them for a long time. >> I alluded to this, but maybe just to kind of expand on it a little bit more. One of the things that we have been doing over the last few years is really exploring areas of the market where we are maybe we hadn't historically participated. And because there are talented people in so many different areas and um not being I guess too dogmatic about what works and what doesn't work and actually being really open-minded that we may find things in places that maybe historically we hadn't and let's go revisit those. And that has opened up a lot of opportunity for us. And so we can still use the playbook of go find best-in-class managers. That has not changed. But if as part of that playbook you say, "Okay, but the areas in which you can do that have expanded because we're open to different kinds of strategies." That actually creates a lot of opportunity. >> So as you built out building blocks with technology, how are you thinking about the next leg with AI? >> Oh my gosh, I wish I had a great answer for you. Um it's so fun. We're having so much fun thinking about this challenge and I think again, really lucky that we have a team that is super open-minded about what technology can bring to our process. So I think we're going to figure this out in a really exciting way. But we are very much right now kind of in a place of um learning as much as we can. I think there's a couple things. One is the technology is changing so quickly that in some ways it feels really almost premature to put a big stake in the ground, right? And make some big investment or process change when in a quarter from now the tools that are available to us could look entirely different or 10 times better. And so we are doing a lot of experimenting right now without like fundamentally redoing things. Um but our team is super eager about like lots of new ideas and so just figuring out how do we do things that feel reasonable with at the same time I don't think we need to be quite on the bleeding edge of discovering everything. That's also maybe a risk we don't need to be taking but we cannot afford to be slow. Um and so finding that happy medium um has been fun and it's a it's a project so it's was one of our key goals and objectives for the team for this year. We we have a two-day process in the summer where we set out all our goals and objectives for the year. And so we have multiple different working groups across the team that are focused on different kind of themes within AI. Everybody on the team is on at least one team um if not more because again this is something where I think we cannot leave anybody behind. And we meet regularly to kind of talk about work streams and progress and what we're learning and what we should be trying and um just sharing a lot of insights and it's it's fun. It's interesting. It's exciting. I don't think we have the answer yet but we're working on it. >> What have you found so far? >> In terms of our use cases? Um I think you know probably like the obvious use case is um just you know the LLMs and their ability to help enhance our diligence processes um to get up to speed more quickly on a particular sector. I've had I had a really interesting quote-unquote conversation recently with um my LLM on what was going on in private credit and where the risks were and how we really should be thinking about the questions we need to be talking for example to our buyout managers about and that was really helpful for me um in kind of preparing me for some conversations. I'll share a funny story if you'll indulge me and you can cut this out if you want about preparing for the conversation with you today which is I didn't have a lot of time this weekend to cram but one of the things that I was able to do was take a handful of the podcast that you've done with other CIO's that I admire and basically said to her I'm going to be talking to Ted this week. I need your help preparing. Here's some examples of interviews he's done in the past. Can you practice an interview with me? And it was extremely effective in helping prepare me for this conversation today and so those are the kinds of use cases that I think we can we will continue very much to pursue. It's like somebody discovered recently that it's great at suggesting off sheet references. Which like really interesting. We found some really good off sheet references just by kind of saying this is who we're doing diligence on like who who in my network would you talk to? Those are examples. I don't think they're revolutionary but we're we're using it and we're excited. >> So what were the most intriguing questions the LM told you I was going to ask you? >> Uh It It didn't tell me intriguing questions. It was very good at summarizing your style. I was like Ted is a very conversational and but very good at asking an opening question and then probing deeper. He also loves anecdotes so the more you can give like colorful examples that makes it much like your answers much more powerful. Like it just felt like I had you know a little bit of like a presentation coach with me. >> Yeah. >> So >> What are some of the things that you feel like you can't do because of a seven person investment team that you might like to if you had a little bit more resources and trying to cover the world? >> You know things that we don't do right now that we've wondered should we do more of? For example, we don't do co-invest, we don't underwrite direct investments. Um we just don't have the skill set. We're at 4 and 1/2 billion. We're probably still at a scale where that's okay, but at some point in time like that may be a muscle that we need to build. Another thing we talk a lot about, but we really haven't done much of is participate in the secondary market, whether it's buying or selling our pieces of our existing portfolio or buying pieces of other portfolios. Because that takes a lot of time and work and effort and bandwidth and we again haven't that hasn't historically been part of our practice. And um so that's another thing for example that we talk about maybe maybe that's a muscle we should be building more as a lot of people seem to have. So, it's a couple examples. >> When you bring this all together, what does success look and feel like to you over time? >> I think it's pretty simple. It's making money for the college. It's doing a great job for Williams. I just that is absolutely our North Star. We want to put up really strong performance. We are competitive about what we're doing here. And we know what we're doing is it's really important to the college. Um it really matters and I think we're all big believers in the mission and the work that the college is doing and so performance. >> What are you hoping to lean into over the next couple years to make that happen? >> It's interesting because I think it can be a little bit daunting um to know what our to understand our mandate and to think about it in big picture terms, like what we need to be able to do. Um and so whenever I get daunted by the task at hand, um the best thing for me to do is come back to well, what is it that we can do today to work towards that goal? And so really this is not going to sound like an earth-shattering answer, but it's showing up every day, getting to work with a beside a great team, and making really good decisions, talking everything through, double-checking our work, hustling, and I think this this team is really well positioned to do all of that, but it is just going to be the accumulation of really hard work day in and day out that is going to lead to great results. There isn't going to be some magic bullet or asset class or manager that we're going to come across that's going to be the answer to it all. It's just going to be that that compounding of great work. >> Well, Abigail, I want to make sure I get a chance to ask you a couple closing questions. >> Okay. >> What is your favorite hobby or activity outside of work and family? >> I'm going to slightly reject the premise of the question because I'm a working mom, so there isn't a lot of hobbies outside of work and family. So, my hobbies are done in conjunction with my family. Um I have two little kids, and they are Suzuki violinists, and um I was a violinist, and so one of my favorite things is um getting to practice with them. It's not always fun. There are sometimes our tears, um but when I get to take out my violin and we get to play music together, it's really special. >> What creates the tears? >> Oh, you have to practice violin every day, and you'd rather be playing outside on the playground or watching the Red Sox or this is really hard, right? Learning an instrument when you're 8 years old is hard, and so it takes but these are the life lessons that I hope they learn from it, right? You just consistency and discipline and breaking down a problem into its components. >> What's the best advice you ever received? >> So, there's a quote that I like um that I have taped to my desk um which I'll just repeat. It's uh life is short, and we do not have much time, so make haste to be kind and be swift to love. >> That's good. How do you bring [snorts] that into the office? >> I think it's the recognition the appreciation of everybody's humanity, right? So, this is a team that shows up every day and works hard. And um when I can see them as like their their whole selves and um they can bring their best selves to work and we can do really really great work together. >> All right, last one. What life lesson have you learned that you wish you knew a lot earlier in life? >> I think just enjoy the ride, honestly. Like it's it kind of goes back to what I just said, right? Which is we don't have a lot of time and um it's a I I'm really a believer it's all going to work out. And so trying not to sweat too much. Not That's not to say we don't take it seriously, but um trusting the process and that things do have a way of working out and let's, you know, enjoy what we get to do cuz we're really lucky to get to be here, to get to do this job and um I think just remembering that is really important. >> Abigail, thanks so much for taking the time. >> Thank you. It's been such a pleasure. I really appreciate the opportunity. >> [music]

transcript_segments.text: [music]
>> Abigail, thanks so much for joining me.
>> Oh, it's so great to be with you today.
Thanks, Ted.
>> I would love you to take me through
the highlights of your career, which I
know aren't going to take that long in
terms of steps, and then we can dive in
from there.
>> Well, so I graduated from Williams in
2005, and from there was very lucky to
get a position at Cambridge Associates
right out of Williams. And
interestingly, I had gotten interested
in the economics of higher education as
a senior in college. I was really
fortunate to get to take a tutorial with
our then president of the college, which
is a gentleman by the name of Morty
Shapiro.
And he was an economist, and so that
really opened my eyes to this whole
world of higher education and finance.
And so that's what interested me in
going to Cambridge Associates.
I was there for 2 years, and I like to
say kind of right place, right time. I
was very fortunate to be a very junior
analyst that happened to be staffed on
the Williams account.
And it happened to overlap with the
period of time where the college had
made a choice to hire its first
dedicated chief investment officer, of
course, Klitch Shelton.
And so when she came on board, um I
started working pretty closely with her.
She didn't have much of a team at the
time. I think if you listen to her
podcast, you'll know I think there was a
joke about her having a computer and a
phone and not much else. So,
got to work really closely with her. And
so when she finally had office space in
kind of spring of 2007, I ended up
joining her here at the Williams
Investment Office.
And have basically been here ever since
except for a 2-year break for business
school.
>> Take me through
20 years of growth inside the office.
What you knew, what you came to learn
along the way about the investment
process and you know, researching
managers, everything.
>> Yeah, I mean, it's a tremendous amount
of growth both for me personally as well
as for our portfolio and what it is that
we do today. So, there's a there will be
a lot to unpack there. But, when we
first got here back in 2007, you know,
the history of the office prior to that
was everything was managed by a
volunteer committee. And so, really
early on, one of the things that we did
was to put in place a policy portfolio.
And there were a lot of manager
decisions that we had to go through. And
I So, for me being here even as an
analyst early on was just such an
eye-opening experience um getting to
see, you know, we did everything from a
$400 million transition
from a separately managed account that
we had to stuffing the FedEx envelopes
and everything in between those two
tasks. And I remember setting up a lot
of the performance reports. Frankly,
some of them we still use today. So,
it's really fun to know, you know,
sometimes people will say to me, you
know, this is how this report works and
I just sort of chuckle and say, yep, I'm
sure it works still exactly the same. I
know what the ins and outs are, but um
we have just I think continued to
develop as an investment organization so
much during that time. Um I think when
we first started, it was 1.3 billion.
Today, we're about 4.5 billion in
assets. And we've just accrued a lot of
knowledge over time about what works for
the portfolio. We've made a lot of
mistakes, but we've also made, you know,
some really, really good decisions along
the way.
>> If you look at your first couple of
years
and just take the the unit of
researching a manager, thinking about a
decision,
what did you
know or not know that you kind of
learned in the next phase? So,
step by step all the way through.
>> Well, I didn't know a lot early on
personally. And I remember so I think I
mentioned I was here for a year before I
went to business school. So I came back
to the investment office in 2010.
And I remember sitting down with
Colette. I'd gone out to lunch with her.
It was my second year of business school
and I had a couple job offers and I was
actually genuinely sitting down to ask
her advice about which one I might take.
And she said, "Well, I'd really like for
you to come back to the investment
office. Would you consider doing that?"
And I said, "Yes, I'd love to." And she
said, "Okay, why don't you write the job
description for the job that you'd like
to do and then we'll talk about it." And
I remember going home and writing that
job description and there were
I think probably three key things that
were on that job description. One was I
wanted to own asset classes and I'll
come back to what those were cuz that's
really the answer to your question. Two
is I wanted to manage people.
Um so that's hence the analyst program.
And three was I wanted to report to
Colette cuz I knew that was going to
really be my opportunity to learn a lot.
But the going back to my first point
around the asset classes that I wanted
to manage, I think what she
basically did was went to the two
managing directors at the time. Um one
on the marketable, one on the
non-marketable side and said, "Okay,
which asset class do you want to give
up?" And so I like to think that I ended
up with great asset classes, but I think
they were basically the ones nobody
wanted which meant that I was managing
investment grade fixed income and real
assets. And so to answer your question,
I really had frankly not a lot of
background in either of those areas of
the market and actually those are really
technical asset classes when you start
to peel back the layers of the onion.
And so I had a lot to learn and I think
that was frankly one of my greatest
lessons was figuring out how to really
dive deep and learn an area of the
market. And so it started with a lot of
conferences. I remember going to a PIMCO
bond boot camp for a week. They used to
run this amazing boot camp, and I spent
a week out in California, and Bill Gross
was there, and they had all their senior
PMs basically like teaching us
everything there was to know about
bonds. So, that was my indoctrination
into the world of fixed income. And then
similarly with oil and gas, you know, I
I grew up in New England, right? I
really didn't know what was going on.
So, that meant I needed to go spend a
lot of time in Texas, right? And so,
there also was this great program down I
think it was at SMU, and they used to
call it oil and gas investing for
non-petroleum engineers. And it was a
week-long course, and it was the same
kind of thing where we would sit in in a
classroom, we'd look at well reports,
they would basically tell us what we're
supposed to be looking at.
I learned a ton from that, and from
networking, there were so many great
people along the way who were really
established in those asset classes that
really, you know, helped me along. And
so, yeah, that was really my first
experience kind of how did how to dig in
in a market, how to dig in on managers,
and it set me up for kind of what I
ended up doing later.
>> Once you built that technical knowledge,
you could say even in those two asset
classes, and then started applying it to
understanding who's good in the space,
what did you think you know, and then
what did you come to learn about
managers to get to the point where you
were comfortable that
you felt like you knew what you were
doing?
>> One of the biggest lessons that I
learned, and somebody said this to me
along the way, and I really try to think
about it a lot, is that you can teach
people what they're supposed to do, but
it's really hard to teach people what
they're not supposed to do without them
actually making the mistakes themselves.
And I think maybe one of the things that
I'm very grateful for was how long of a
leash I had to kind of figure out some
of the mistakes and that's maybe where I
had the greatest lessons, but
I don't know. There's so many different
things that I would say. Um,
you know, one of them is when it's very
clear that there's something great,
chances are good you should just go for
it. And in this business, we don't get
points for difficulty.
We get points for making money, right?
And so, you don't necessarily have to
always go find the least discovered
idea. Um, if somebody's recommending a
manager to you over and over again, for
example, like just probably like worth
taking that advice and at least spending
a lot of time on it.
Um, so that might have been one of my
lessons learned. I think the other thing
is real assets was not a place where
quite as many people were spending a ton
of time, and so the managers were so
generous with me and the time that they
gave me, and it really got me
comfortable like sitting in a conference
room for hours upon hours upon hours and
just
constantly asking as many questions as I
possibly could. And they were all so
patient with me, and that really taught
me a lot, too. So, those are maybe some
of my lessons is, you know, not being
afraid to ask questions, to take their
time when you need it. Um, and you're
going to make some mistakes.
>> How did Collette work with you to allow
you to make mistakes, but then not have
it impact a portfolio when there aren't
really that many decisions that get
made?
>> She was always really good at like I
would do a lot of the legwork, right?
The sourcing, the landscaping, the
networking,
coming up with a short list of ideas,
and she always met with every single one
of our managers before they went into
the portfolio, and she was very good
that one of the one of the many things
she was very good at is whenever you put
a manager in front of her, you know, she
would flip to immediately like page 47
of I don't know how she developed this
instinct, but she could get to page 47
within the first 5 minutes and be like
this is the issue, right? And so she
would come with me kind of for those
final diligence meetings and that's
really where a lot of I would get a lot
of feedback on, you know, this we're
probably going to lean in this
direction, right? So she would kind of
towards the end of the process I think
really help drive the direction of where
we were going and it would that taught
me a lot.
>> How did you evolve from covering these
two asset classes to broadening across
portfolio?
>> So from there I think probably the
biggest
next step that I took was ultimately
taking over our hedge fund asset classes
which
for those that maybe know less about the
Williams portfolio, those have always
been really important asset classes for
our portfolio. We've been long-term
investors in hedge funds and
they represent I think at the time they
might have represented 40% of the
portfolio. But again, I think because I
had built a little bit of a muscle
around how to learn to do something new,
right? Stepping into the complexity of
long short equity and absolute return,
that was again kind of that same
opportunity to figure out, okay, how do
I unpack this whole landscape? How do I
get to know these managers?
There's some more sensitivities, right?
In hedge funds around like how much time
you're taking with managers and things
like that. So I think
I had to I had to learn to sharpen my
diligence process a little bit which was
probably a really good evolution. It was
a very different network, too. I had to
there were new people that I needed to
talk to both from the LP community as
well as the GP community and that also
really expanded my horizons.
>> So in the years before you took over as
CIO,
what changed in the thinking on the
investment portfolio here?
>> It's interesting you ask that cuz we're
doing we're in the midst of our asset
allocation work right now. And so it's
always a really good opportunity to stop
and reflect. And I have been
looking a lot at the portfolio over
time. And what's interesting to me is
the actually the big blocks of what we
do really haven't changed, right? There
are on the margin of course changes that
we're making every year.
But I think one of the things that has
worked really well for Williams is that
we've been extremely consistent in how
we deploy the portfolio, what works for
Williams, and really keeping at the
center what we do, why is it that we're
here?
And we're we're very clear-eyed about
that. You know, our job is to earn a 5%
real return, to make sure that we're
doing that with the appropriate level of
risk that we're taking, and ensuring
that we have sufficient liquidity
because we support nearly 60% of the
operating budget for the college. And so
yes, those numbers have changed on the
margin like the the percentage of the
operating budget support, for example,
but
our goals have never changed. And so in
some ways the way that we pursue that
has always remained very consistent. And
I think that's a real source of strength
for us.
>> How do you think about
your strategy and almost like your right
to win as an LP? What do you bring to
the table as the next LP in a fund that
you're interested investing in?
>> There are a couple different things that
we will talk about with a manager while
we're meeting with them and and
hopefully getting access to something
that may be more access constrained. So
one is the mission, right? That really
does resonate with people. Williams is
extremely generous with its financial
aid program. We have no loans in our
financial aid program at this point in
time because of the generosity that
comes from the endowment and our donors.
